originalTitle: 'Speed, money and compassion' - lessons from an Ebola survivor and other experts
title: Learning from Our Heroes: Staying Safe from Sickness!
content:
Jambo, amazing kids of Africa! I'm your friendly news reporter, and I have a very important story for you today. It's about staying strong and healthy, and how we can learn from brave people who faced a big sickness a long time ago.

Hello, Friends! What is This Story About?

This story is about a sickness called Ebola. It's a very serious sickness caused by tiny, tiny germs that you cannot see. When many people get sick at the same time, we call it an ‘outbreak’ or an ‘epidemic’. Imagine lots of kids in many schools getting the same big tummy ache – that's a bit like an outbreak! Ten years ago, a sickness called Ebola made many people in some parts of West Africa very, very sick. It was a tough time. But guess what? Many people got better! They are called 'survivors'. And these survivors, along with clever doctors and nurses, have important lessons for us today. Did you know? Germs are so small, you can only see them with a special powerful microscope!

What is Ebola Sickness Like?

Ebola is not like a common cold or a little cough. It makes people very unwell. They might get a high fever, feel very tired, have a tummy ache, or feel sick to their stomach. It's a very serious sickness that needs special care from doctors and nurses. It's important to know that doctors and nurses are like superheroes. They work very hard to help sick people get better and to stop the germs from spreading to others. Did you know? Our bodies are amazing! They have special 'soldiers' inside called white blood cells that fight off germs and help us get well.

Learning from Ten Years Ago

A long time ago, when most of you were just babies or not even born yet, many people in countries like Liberia, Sierra Leone, and Guinea got sick with Ebola. It was a very scary time for families and communities. But during that tough time, people learned so much! They learned how to take care of each other, how to stop the sickness from spreading, and how to help people get better. These lessons are like precious gifts. They help us know what to do if a similar big sickness ever comes again. We are strong because we learn from our past. Did you know? Ten years is a whole decade! That's a lot of birthdays and a lot of growing up!

Lesson 1: Be Quick!

One of the most important lessons is to be very quick! If someone starts to feel sick, especially with a high fever or a very bad tummy ache, they must tell a grown-up right away. This could be a parent, a teacher, or another trusted adult. Why is being quick so important? Because the faster doctors know someone is sick, the faster they can help them. And the faster they help, the better chance the sick person has of getting well. Also, being quick helps stop the tiny germs from spreading to other people. Did you know? Telling a grown-up when you don't feel well is a very brave and smart thing to do!

Lesson 2: We Need Special Tools!

Imagine trying to build a house without any bricks or wood. It would be impossible, right? It's the same for fighting sickness. Doctors and nurses need special tools and things to do their job safely and well. They need special suits to protect themselves from the germs. They need clean water, good soap, and special medicines. They also need clean hospitals or clinics where they can care for sick people safely. These tools cost money, and it’s important that communities and governments make sure our health heroes have everything they need. When they have the right tools, everyone is safer. Did you know? Some special doctor suits look like astronaut suits! They help keep germs away.

Lesson 3: Be Kind and Caring!

This might be the most important lesson of all. When people are sick, they don't just need medicine; they need kindness and care. Sometimes, when a big sickness is around, people can get scared. They might even be afraid of sick people or their families. But our heroes, the survivors and experts, teach us to be compassionate. That means being kind, understanding, and helpful. People who are sick need to feel loved, not lonely or forgotten. Being kind means we help families who are going through a tough time. It means we don't tease or make fun of anyone who is unwell. It means we stand together as a community. Did you know? Giving someone a warm smile or a kind word can make them feel much better, even if they are very sick!

How Can You Help Today?

Even though you are kids, you can be part of the solution! Here are some simple things you can do: * **Wash your hands often!** Use soap and clean water. Sing your favourite song while you wash for at least 20 seconds. This washes away tiny germs! * **Tell a grown-up** if you feel sick or if a friend looks unwell. * **Listen to your parents and teachers.** They know what is best for your health and safety. * **Be kind to everyone.** Share your toys, help a friend, and use nice words. A kind community is a strong community. Did you know? Washing your hands properly is like putting on an invisible shield against germs!

Together We Are Strong!

So, my wonderful African friends, remember these lessons. Learning from the past helps us be prepared for the future. By being quick, making sure our doctors have tools, and always being kind, we can keep ourselves and our communities safe and strong. Let’s all work together to stay healthy and happy! Thank you for listening to these important lessons. Keep shining bright!
summary: Experts and survivors of the Ebola outbreak from ten years ago are sharing vital lessons about acting quickly, having the right tools, and showing kindness to help communities stay safe from sickness.
whyMatters: This is important because learning from people who were very sick with Ebola and got better, along with clever doctors, teaches us how to keep everyone safe and healthy if a big sickness like that happens again. It helps us be prepared and kind.

originalTitle: Red Cross volunteers die from suspected Ebola in DR Congo
title: Red Cross volunteers die from suspected Ebola in DR Congo
content: They are thought to have caught the virus before the outbreak was identified, the Red Cross says.

originalTitle: Senegal's president sacks PM and former ally after months-long feud
title: Big News in Senegal: A New Team for the Country!
content:
Hello from Sunny Senegal!

Hello, amazing friends! It's your friendly news reporter, here to share some interesting news from a beautiful country in Africa called Senegal. Senegal is a place full of sunshine, yummy food, and happy people. Have you ever heard of it? It's on the west side of Africa, right by the big blue ocean!

A Big Job: The President

Every country has a special leader. This leader is like the captain of a big ship, or the head of a very large family – the whole country! In Senegal, this special leader is called the President. His job is to help make good choices for everyone in the country, so people can live well and be happy. The President helps decide about schools, hospitals, and even what kinds of toys might be made! **Did you know?** The capital city of Senegal is called Dakar. It's a very busy and colorful city!

Meet President Bassirou Diomaye Faye

Our story today is about Senegal's President, a kind man named Bassirou Diomaye Faye. He is the new President, and he has a very important job. He works very hard every day to make Senegal a wonderful place for all the children and grown-ups.

A President's Helper: The Prime Minister

Now, imagine you have a very big project at school, like building a tall tower with blocks. You can't do it all by yourself, can you? You need helpers! A President also needs helpers. One of his most important helpers is called the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister is like the President's chief assistant, helping to make sure everything gets done smoothly. They work very closely together.

A Change in the Team

Sometimes, even the best of friends or the best of teams might have different ideas about how to do things. It's like when you and your friend want to play two different games. You both want to have fun, but you have different ideas! Well, something like that happened with President Bassirou Diomaye Faye and his Prime Minister. They had been working together for some time, but they started to have different ideas about how to lead the country. This is called having a "disagreement" or a "feud" when it goes on for a while. **Did you know?** A "feud" just means people have been disagreeing or having arguments for a long time. It doesn't mean they are bad people, just that they see things differently!

Saying "Goodbye" to a Helper

So, because they had different ideas, President Bassirou Diomaye Faye made a big decision. He decided it was time for a change. He asked his Prime Minister to step down from his job. This is sometimes called "sacking" or "firing" someone, but it just means asking them to leave their position so someone new can come in. It was a big step for the President. He hopes that by changing his main helper, he can find new ways to make Senegal even better.

The Whole Team Changes!

But wait, there's more! The President didn't just ask the Prime Minister to leave. He also said that the whole "government" needs to change. What is a "government"? Imagine your school has different teachers for different subjects like math, art, and reading. And there's a principal who leads them all. The government is like that big team of teachers and helpers who work with the President to run the country. They help build roads, make sure there are enough schools, and even help people get better when they are sick. When the President "dissolves the government," it means he's saying "okay everyone, we need a new team!" All the people who were helping in different ways will stop their jobs for now, and the President will choose new people to help him. It's like changing all the players on a football team to try a new strategy. **Did you know?** Governments around the world do many important jobs, like making sure you have clean water to drink and safe places to play!

Feeling a Little Unsure: Uncertainty

When big changes happen, sometimes people feel a little bit unsure about what will happen next. This feeling is called "uncertainty." It's like when you finish a really fun book and you don't know what book you'll read next. You might be a little curious or even a tiny bit worried. In Senegal, because the President made these big changes, some people might feel a bit unsure about what will happen in their country next. They are waiting to see who the new helpers will be and what new plans the President will make.

What is "Debt"?

There's another important thing to understand. Sometimes, countries, just like people, need to borrow money. When a country borrows money, it's called having "debt." It's like if you borrow a toy from a friend, you have a "toy debt" and you need to give it back later! Sometimes, countries borrow a lot of money to build new roads, hospitals, or schools. But if they borrow too much, it can become a big problem, like having too many toys to return! This is called a "debt crisis." Senegal is trying to be very careful with its money right now. **Did you know?** Many countries around the world borrow money to help their people, but it's important to manage it wisely!

Why Are These Changes Important?

These changes are very important because the people who lead a country make choices that affect everyone. When the President chooses new helpers, he wants to choose people who he thinks will do the very best job for Senegal. He wants to solve the money problems and make sure everyone has a good future. It's like choosing the right ingredients for a delicious meal – you want the best ones to make the meal taste great!

Looking Ahead: New Beginnings!

So, what happens next? President Bassirou Diomaye Faye will now choose a new Prime Minister and a new team of helpers for the government. He will pick people who he believes will work well together to help Senegal grow strong and happy. It's a new beginning! Changes can sometimes feel a bit strange, but they often lead to new chances and new ideas. **Did you know?** Senegal is famous for its delicious dish called Thieboudienne (pronounced Cheh-boo-jen), which is fish, rice, and vegetables! Yummy!

Learning Together in Africa!

So, there you have it! A big change in the government of our friend Senegal. It reminds us that even grown-ups in charge sometimes have to make big decisions. But the goal is always to make life better for everyone, especially the children. We'll keep watching to see the new team President Faye chooses and how they work to make Senegal even brighter! That's all for today from your African news reporter! Keep smiling, keep learning, and remember that Africa is full of amazing stories!
summary: President Bassirou Diomaye Faye of Senegal has made a big change by choosing a new main helper, the Prime Minister, and a new team for the whole government to make the country better.
whyMatters: This is important because a country's leaders make choices that affect everyone, like building schools and helping people, so having the best team is key for Senegal's future.
